blurb: The iconic shortgrass-prairie native: a fine blue-green turf topped with quirky one-sided 'eyebrow' comb seedheads that bleach blond and stand through winter.
size: 12–18 in tall in seed × 12 in wide (foliage 6–8 in)
water: Low; xeric once established — no supplemental water needed
spread: Clumping warm-season grass; can knit into a low meadow turf
seasons: Blue-green fine turf in summer; horizontal comb seedheads summer into fall; blond and persistent through winter
wildlife: Larval host for skipper butterflies; seeds feed birds; warm-season prairie cover
toxic: Non-toxic; a valuable native forage grass

care.hardiness: Bone-hardy on the Front Range: a major warm-season grass of the western Great Plains whose range runs north into the Canadian prairie (Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba), and in lab tests its crowns survived two months at -31°F, so Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters are no concern.[1][2] The Plant Select 'Blonde Ambition' selection is rated USDA zones 4–9 and to about 7,500 ft.[4]
care.planting: A warm-season (C4) grass, so set out plugs or container stock in late spring once the soil has warmed past the mid-May last frost — it greens up late (growth begins when soil at 6 in averages ~52°F) and resents being planted into cold ground.[2] Direct-seeding is best done April to mid-May in the central Great Plains.[1] In containers use a gritty, fast-draining mix, water more often than in-ground (pots dry fast), and avoid winter wet; the species is far hardier in the ground than in an exposed pot.[1][4]
care.sun: Full sun — blue grama is intolerant of shade and wants the open exposure of its native shortgrass prairie; our intense high-altitude sun suits it.[1]
care.soil: Grows on soils from sandy to clayey and tolerates the alkaline, calcareous ground common around Boulder — it is rarely found on even weakly acid soils and shows fair salinity tolerance — but wants good drainage and is not tolerant of frequent flooding or submergence.[1][2] Lean, low-organic soil is ideal; growth is less vigorous on true sands or heavy clays.[1][3]
care.water: Low; xeric once established. One of the most drought-tolerant native grasses, it uses water rapidly when available then goes dormant in dry spells and can survive on as little as 7–8 in of annual precipitation, so it needs little to no supplemental water here after the first season.[2][3]
care.spacing: Space plants about 12–18 in apart to knit into a low turf or meadow; the larger 'Blonde Ambition' selection reaches 30–36 in and wants more room.[3][4]
care.propagation: Easiest from seed (the cultivars run ~700,000–800,000 seeds/lb); broadcast or drill onto a firm, weed-free bed and press in for good seed-to-soil contact.[1] Establishing clumps can also be lifted and divided in spring as growth resumes. Seedlings are drought-sensitive their first weeks — keep the surface moist a few days at a time until adventitious roots form (within 6–10 weeks of emergence), or they may die.[1]
care.sow: Sow at a depth of ¼ to ½ inch on a firm, weed-free seedbed; suitable seeding dates here are April to mid-May.[1]
care.depth: Sow ¼ to ½ inch deep.[1]
care.bloom: A foliage grass grown for its quirky one-sided 'eyebrow' comb seedheads rather than flowers; it heads out July through fall, the combs bleaching blond and standing through winter.[2][3]
care.maintenance: Very low care. Cut the clump back to a few inches in late winter or very early spring before new growth begins — because it is warm-season it greens up late, so don't be alarmed by bare clumps into May.[2][4] Leave the seedheads for winter structure and birds; mowing or controlled cutting also handles any weeds.[1]
care.troubles: Largely trouble-free; grasshoppers can graze stands and white-grub larvae may feed on roots, with occasional leaf, tar-spot, and rust diseases reported — none usually serious in a garden.[1]
care.companions: A natural with other shortgrass-prairie natives — it grows in mixed stands with buffalograss, needle-and-thread, and western wheatgrass in the wild — and pairs well with xeric forbs like blanket flower, prairie zinnia, and dotted gayfeather in a dry meadow planting.[1]

edible.summary: A valuable native forage grass, not a human food — no part is eaten by people.[1][2]
edible.caution: Blue grama is a highly palatable, nutritious, non-toxic forage grass that feeds livestock year-round (curing well on the stem) and whose seeds feed birds and small mammals, but there is no documented human food use of any part — so it is treated as inedible here. It is not poisonous; the absence of food documentation simply means it is grown as turf, forage, and ornament, not for eating.[1][2]
